DOVA introduces deliberation-first orchestration: before any tool call, a meta-reasoning layer consults a persistent user model and entity-aware context, cutting inference cost 40–60 % on routine tasks while preserving depth for hard ones.

The Edmonds City Council has postponed until next week its vote on confirming Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator. Lucash, currently deputy city manager of Kenmore, would receive an annual base salary of $226,068 if confirmed.
A new statewide housing law takes effect June 11 that will require cities to expand where housing can be built for people experiencing homelessness.

The City of Edmonds provided an update on ongoing efforts to resolve operational issues at the wastewater treatment plant. The $31 million carbon recovery system has experienced significant delays and technical failures since construction began in 2021.

The US-Iran conflict continues to escalate with a 'war of blockades' over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has seized two cargo ships, including the Epaminondas, and stated that reopening the strait is 'impossible' if the US blockade continues.

The EU has approved a €90bn loan for Ukraine after Ukraine resumed pumping Russian oil through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia, ending months of deadlock. This funding is seen as vital EU support for Kyiv, with two-thirds earmarked for defense needs.

China's economy, which weathered Trump's tariffs, is now being significantly impacted by the Iran war. The conflict is putting pressure on factory orders, costs, and jobs in China's export-driven economy, with workers in manufacturing hubs like Foshan struggling with reduced opportunities.

Doctors Without Borders (MSF) has accused Israel of manufacturing a malnutrition crisis in Gaza through restrictions on food and aid deliveries. Their analysis shows over half of pregnant women suffered malnutrition, with 90% of babies born premature and 84% having low birth weight.

Gulf states are pushing a UN Security Council resolution threatening Iran with sanctions if it doesn't halt attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz and disclose mine locations. Qatar's ambassador emphasized this is crucial for global economic stability and energy security.
The US has imposed new sanctions on Cuba targeting the military-controlled GAESA conglomerate, amid what UN experts call an 'energy starvation' campaign.

In 'The One Hundred Milliseconds Between the World and You', Maria Popova distills Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection to a single insight: perception is not a passive intake of data but a creative act that mirrors the self.
Diane Ackerman’s 'A Cosmic Pastoral' frames the universe as an improbable love letter, asking how a system can observe itself. The answer, she proposes, is 'systematic wonder'.

Aryeh Neier’s 'The New War on Speech' dissects Trump’s January 2026 executive order purporting to end 'government censorship'. Neier, former ACLU director, traces the order’s contradiction: while claiming to restore free speech, it targets non-citizens and revives 1917-18 Espionage Act tactics.
Tim Judah’s 'A Bitter Winter in Ukraine' reports from Kyiv on the war’s third winter, where novelist Andrey Kurkov’s resigned observation—'the future is just too far away'—captures national fatigue.

Flora Champy’s 'The role of literature as the key to personal freedom' reframes Proust’s 1905 essay 'On Reading' as a defense of literature against utilitarian moralizing. Proust, translating Ruskin’s 'Sesame and Lilies', rejects the Victorian notion that books must 'pay' through moral improvement.

triadic_ontology.findings: Three 2025 works advance triadic/relational metaphysics: Migliorini unites Trinitarian theology with analytic relational ontology; Slade proposes a SU(3)-based quantum ontology where qutrits are ontic primitives and triadic mediation is enforced by ℤ₃ rotations; and the Karl Alber volume "Trinitarian Ontologies" inaugurates a new series bridging analytic and continental approaches to triadic philosophy, gathering key scholars to map the field’s theological roots and contemporary applications.

ai_systems.findings: Three new papers define the 2026 autonomous-research stack. DOVA introduces deliberation-first orchestration: before any tool call, a meta-reasoning layer consults a persistent user model and entity-aware context, cutting inference cost 40–60 % on routine tasks while preserving depth for hard ones. MARS adds Budget-Aware Planning—cost-constrained Monte-Carlo tree search that balances performance against execution expense—and Comparative Reflective Memory that mines cross-branch insights; 63 % of its “Aha!” moments come from transfer between search paths. Mimosa completes the trio with an evolving multi-agent framework that auto-synthesizes task-specific workflows, scores them with an LLM judge, and iteratively refines the topology; on ScienceAgentBench it pushes DeepSeek-V3.2 to 43.1 % success, beating both single-agent and static multi-agent baselines. Meanwhile, RASPRef shows how prompts themselves can be optimized: retrieval-augmented, self-supervised prompt refinement treats the pr [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Exa returned three official and news items: a November 2024 House joint hearing titled “Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena: Exposing the Truth” featuring Luis Elizondo and other witnesses; a 1 April 2026 press release from Rep. Anna Paulina Luna’s Oversight Task Force demanding UAP videos from the Department of War and citing whistleblower claims of withheld footage; and a 30 April 2026 NewsNation interview in which Luna says she has personally seen evidence of “nonhuman origin” in classified SCIF briefings and plans a public release once declassified. Together they show ongoing congressional pressure for UAP disclosure extending into 2026, with explicit references to non-human intelligence.

weather: Partly cloudy this morning, becoming overcast by afternoon. High 58°F, low 47°F. Light rain possible after 3 pm, 30 % chance. Winds west-southwest 8–12 mph, gusting to 18 mph near the Sound. Evening stays gray with patchy drizzle and temps easing to the upper 40s—good weather for a light jacket if you’re heading to the waterfront.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council has postponed until next week its vote on confirming Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator. Lucash, currently deputy city manager of Kenmore, would receive an annual base salary of $226,068 if confirmed. The council also advanced a measure to reactivate the volunteer salary commission, which would make binding salary recommendations for elected officials. Mayor Mike Rosen broke a 3-3 tie to move the salary commission proposal forward.

A new statewide housing law takes effect June 11 that will require cities to expand where housing can be built for people experiencing homelessness. The law has sparked mixed reactions from local leaders and organizations in Edmonds, with some expressing concerns about implementation challenges while others see it as necessary to address the housing crisis.

The City of Edmonds provided an update on ongoing efforts to resolve operational issues at the wastewater treatment plant. The $31 million carbon recovery system has experienced significant delays and technical failures since construction began in 2021. The city has brought in Jacobs Engineering as an independent consultant to help address the problems, with recent trial runs showing mixed results. The city has spent over $5 million hauling wastewater solids to Oregon landfills while the system remains non-operational.

global_news.findings: The US-Iran conflict continues to escalate with a 'war of blockades' over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has seized two cargo ships, including the Epaminondas, and stated that reopening the strait is 'impossible' if the US blockade continues. Meanwhile, Pakistan is pushing for peace talks between the US and Iran, though no confirmed date has been set for negotiations.

The EU has approved a €90bn loan for Ukraine after Ukraine resumed pumping Russian oil through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia, ending months of deadlock. This funding is seen as vital EU support for Kyiv, with two-thirds earmarked for defense needs.

China's economy, which weathered Trump's tariffs, is now being significantly impacted by the Iran war. The conflict is putting pressure on factory orders, costs, and jobs in China's export-driven economy, with workers in manufacturing hubs like Foshan struggling with reduced opportunities.

Doctors Without Borders (MSF) has accused Israel of manufacturing a malnutrition crisis in Gaza through restrictions on food and aid deliveries. Their analysis shows over half of pregnant women suffered malnutrition, with 90% of babies born premature and 84% having low birth weight.

Gulf states are pushing a UN Security Council resolution threatening Iran with sanctions if it doesn't halt attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz and disclose mine locations. Qatar's ambassador emphasized this is crucial for global economic stability and energy security.

The US has imposed new sanctions on Cuba targeting the military-controlled GAESA conglomerate, amid what UN experts call an 'energy starvation' campaign. The sanctions come as Cuba faces severe fuel shortages affecting hospitals and schools, with only one Russian oil tanker reaching Cuba in recent months.

intellectual_journals.findings: In 'The One Hundred Milliseconds Between the World and You', Maria Popova distills Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection to a single insight: perception is not a passive intake of data but a creative act that mirrors the self. Drawing on neurological case studies of patients whose consciousness enters 'standstills', Sacks argues that motion is perceived not as robotic calculation but as a unique qualitative experience—what Francis Crick and Christof Koch term 'qualia'. These wholly subjective experiences require a 'coalition' of millions of neurons, forming and dissolving in fractions of  [TRUNCATED]

Diane Ackerman’s 'A Cosmic Pastoral' frames the universe as an improbable love letter, asking how a system can observe itself. The answer, she proposes, is 'systematic wonder'. Tracing her discovery of Carl Sagan’s mentorship and his 1974 letter to Timothy Leary enclosing her astronomy-inspired poetry, Ackerman meditates on the weight of cosmic consciousness: the paradox of doing laundry while reckoning with star-forged iron in our blood. She celebrates poets as 'weight-bearers of wonder', tasked with holding the 'cosmic overwhelm'—from Pluto’s 'rock-ribbed' defiance to Jupiter’s 'agitated fos [TRUNCATED]

Aryeh Neier’s 'The New War on Speech' dissects Trump’s January 2026 executive order purporting to end 'government censorship'. Neier, former ACLU director, traces the order’s contradiction: while claiming to restore free speech, it targets non-citizens and revives 1917-18 Espionage Act tactics. The piece juxtaposes Trump’s rhetoric with historical assaults on expression, noting that Wilson’s administration prosecuted 2,000 dissenters under wartime laws. Neier warns that the order’s focus on social media 'coercion' masks a broader chilling effect, particularly on immigrant and international voi [TRUNCATED]

Tim Judah’s 'A Bitter Winter in Ukraine' reports from Kyiv on the war’s third winter, where novelist Andrey Kurkov’s resigned observation—'the future is just too far away'—captures national fatigue. Judah details the grinding assault on Ukraine’s energy grid (60% rely on power stations for heat), linking the frost-resistance of a Kyiv farmer’s banana trees to civilian endurance. The piece traces Ukraine’s evolution from 2014’s distant Donbas conflict to 2026’s existential siege, where drone innovation and civilian mobilization coexist with gambling-addicted soldiers and lukewarm radiators.

Flora Champy’s 'The role of literature as the key to personal freedom' reframes Proust’s 1905 essay 'On Reading' as a defense of literature against utilitarian moralizing. Proust, translating Ruskin’s 'Sesame and Lilies', rejects the Victorian notion that books must 'pay' through moral improvement. Instead, he argues reading is a disinterested communion with minds unconstrained by social performance—'a unique form of communication' that preserves past sensations and makes time tangible. Champy positions this as a rebuke to contemporary cost/benefit analyses of culture, asserting literature’s v [TRUNCATED]

English and History teachers gained four specialized AI co-workers this spring. **Humy.ai** leads with 1,200+ interactive AI-powered historical figures plus 90+ teacher tools, supporting auto-graded assignments across Social Studies, ELA, and World Languages while maintaining FERPA/COPPA compliance. **Twee** provides AI-powered tools specifically for English teachers to create questions, dialogues [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
The actress Ellen Burstyn has what she calls an “inner library” of poetry that she has memorized in the course of her long life. “I’m ninety-three now, so I probably have less in my head than I used to when it comes to poems,” she said the other day. “But some have been there for years. Decades.” Burstyn was in her Upper West Side apartment, which was festooned with books, plants, crystals, orbs, and statues of Shiva and Buddha and Guanyin (“the goddess of compassion,” she noted). Meditative music piped in through the sound system. “I was getting a massage, so it’s left over,” she explained.
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come reading Hafez’s poetry,” she said, recalling a path lined with roses “about at nose height, so as you’re walking you’re sniffing a different rose each step.”
In 1995, she was in “How to Make an American Quilt”; her co-star Maya Angelou would recite poems for her. “The makeup department threw up their hands, because I had to be completely re-made up after lunch every day from crying in Maya’s trailer,” Burstyn recalled. “So then I snuck in and brought Q-tips.” She leaned back in her chair and mimed holding the cotton tips to her eyes. “I would lay down in her trailer with Q-tips and go, ‘O.K., hit me!’ ” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2004 Pulitzer Prize for Fiction, this debut novel reimagines antebellum Virginia through the story of Henry Townsend, a former slave who becomes a slave-owning landowner. Jones crafts a morally intricate narrative that explores the paradox of Black slaveholders and the corrosive legacy of slavery, weaving together the lives of freed and enslaved people with biblical gravity. Critics hailed it as a masterpiece destined for the American literary canon, comparing its scope and moral complexity to Toni Morrison's Beloved and William Faulkner's Absalom, Absalom!
